sections.title: Overview
sections.content: High-quality chest compressions are the cornerstone of resuscitation. They maintain cerebral and coronary perfusion during cardiac arrest, and minimising interruptions is one of the most important modifiable factors in survival.

sections.title: Adult Technique
sections.content:
1. Place the patient supine on a firm surface as soon as practical

2. Position your hands on the centre of the chest on the lower half of the sternum

3. Compress at a rate of 100-120 per minute to a depth of 5-6 cm in adults while allowing full recoil

4. Keep pauses as short as possible for rhythm analysis, defibrillation, airway interventions, or compressor changes

5. Use 30:2 when no advanced airway is in place; once an advanced airway is established, continue compressions with asynchronous ventilations per resuscitation guidance

sections.title: Paediatric Technique
sections.content:
**Infant (< 1 year):**
- Use a two-finger technique for a single rescuer or a two-thumb encircling technique when two rescuers are present
- Compress to approximately one-third of the chest depth
- Follow age-appropriate compression-to-ventilation ratios in line with current resuscitation guidance

**Child (1 year to puberty):**
- Use one or two hands depending on the size of the child and rescuer
- Compress to approximately one-third of the chest depth
- Follow age-appropriate compression-to-ventilation ratios in line with current resuscitation guidance

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Rotate compressors regularly before fatigue causes compression quality to fall
- Use feedback, metronomes, or prompts when available, but do not let devices distract from overall rhythm and sequence management
- Resume compressions immediately after shocks and minimise rhythm-check pauses
- Transport, mechanical CPR, and advanced procedures should not come at the cost of prolonged interruptions
